title: AI Pair Programming — The Copilot Awakens

steps.title: Welcome to the Lab
steps.instruction:
Welcome! In this hands-on lab you'll set up GitHub Copilot and experience inline completions and Copilot Chat first-hand.

Follow the steps in this panel — each one walks you through a real interaction. The glowing edge and arrow will show you where to look.

steps.title: Review the Slides
steps.instruction:
Start by reading through the first few slides in the presentation on the left. They cover what GitHub Copilot is and how it integrates into VS Code.

Use the → arrow key inside the slide panel to advance.

steps.title: Install the Extension
steps.instruction:
Open the Extensions sidebar (Ctrl+Shift+X), search for GitHub Copilot, and click Install.

A single extension now bundles both inline completions and Copilot Chat.

steps.title: Sign In to GitHub
steps.instruction:
Click the Copilot icon in the status bar → Sign in to GitHub. Authorize VS Code in your browser, then come back here.

The icon should turn into the Copilot sparkle when you're active.

steps.title: Open Copilot Chat
steps.instruction: Open the Copilot Chat panel with <kbd>Ctrl+Shift+I</kbd> (or click the chat icon in the Activity Bar). The chat panel will appear on the right side of your screen.

steps.title: Choose Your Model
steps.instruction:
In the Copilot Chat panel, click the model selector dropdown at the top. Different models have different strengths — Claude excels at reasoning, GPT-4o is fast and versatile.

For this lab, any model works. Pick one and continue.

steps.title: Your First Prompt
steps.instruction:
Type a question in the Copilot Chat input box:

What is GitHub Copilot and how does it help developers?

Press Enter and watch the response stream in.

steps.title: Read the Response
steps.instruction:
Read Copilot's response carefully. Notice how it uses code formatting, bullet points, and practical examples.

This is the same AI that powers inline completions — but here you can have a full conversation.

steps.title: Try Inline Completions
steps.instruction:
Open a new file (Ctrl+N), set the language to JavaScript, and type this comment:

// Calculate the total price of items in a shopping cart

Press Enter and watch Copilot suggest the function body as ghost text. Press Tab to accept.

steps.title: Ask for an Explanation
steps.instruction:
Select the function you just generated, then type in Copilot Chat:

/explain

Copilot will break down how the code works step by step.

steps.title: Ask for a Fix
steps.instruction:
Now try /fix — imagine your function doesn't handle empty arrays or missing prices. Copilot will suggest defensive checks.

Review the diff it proposes and click Apply if you like it.

steps.title: Generate Tests
steps.instruction:
Select your function and type /tests in Copilot Chat. It will generate unit tests covering normal cases, edge cases, and error conditions.

You can click Insert into New File to save them.

steps.title: Try the Terminal
steps.instruction:
Open the integrated terminal (Ctrl+`). Try asking Copilot about terminal commands — for example, type @terminal how do I list all git branches? in the chat.

Copilot can help with CLI tasks too!
steps.tip: You can also use Ctrl+I inside the terminal for inline Copilot assistance.

steps.title: Configure Copilot Settings
steps.instruction: Open Settings (Ctrl+,) and search for copilot. Try toggling github.copilot.enable per language — for example, disable Copilot for Markdown but keep it on for JavaScript and Python.

steps.title: Lab Complete!
steps.instruction:
Congratulations! 🎉 You've activated Copilot, used inline completions, explored Chat commands (/explain, /fix, /tests), worked with the terminal, and configured settings.

You're ready for the next course: MCP for You and Me.
steps.tip: Head back to the slides on the left for a recap, or close this panel to explore on your own.
